key_insight: By shaping an embedded void inside dense, ordered grains, the authors induce grain-level anisotropy and softening that can reduce the packing’s apparent shear modulus by up to 90% without requiring auxetic grains.
engineering_relevance: This suggests a geometry-first design route for soft granular metamaterials where macroscopic stiffness is tuned through grain architecture rather than only material composition or packing fraction.
method: Finite element simulations of dense, ordered granular packings under constant solid fraction
Controlled variation of embedded-void geometry to modify grain elastic response under shear
Comparison of macroscopic shear response with contact-force anisotropy across system sizes and friction levels
evidence_signal: The abstract reports up to a 90% reduction in apparent shear modulus, with the effect correlating strongly with decreased contact-force anisotropy and remaining insensitive to system size and contact friction variations.
watch_item: The results are simulation-based for dense, ordered packings, so experimental validation and robustness in disordered real granular assemblies remain important implementation questions.
paper_title: Geometry-mediated shear softening in dense ordered granular packings
